result:
The sky over the Pacific was the color of gunmetal when I first saw the whale breach. It wasn’t a dramatic Hollywood leap—just a slow, deliberate roll of something so immense it made the surface of the ocean look like skin stretched over a muscle that had decided, for one brief second, to flex. A puff of mist hung in the air like a question mark. Then the whale slid back under and the sea stitched itself shut again, as if nothing had happened.

I was twenty-three, standing on the deck of a converted trawler that smelled of diesel and old coffee. The boat was called the *Pelican*, which struck me as optimistic. We were somewhere off the coast of Baja, chasing stories rather than fish. I had a notebook, a cheap waterproof camera, and the unshakable conviction that the world was still mostly undiscovered. That conviction would not survive the decade, but it was useful while it lasted.

Thirty-one years later I am sitting at a wooden table in a house I built with my own hands on an island in the Salish Sea. The cedar planks still smell faintly of resin when the woodstove heats them. Outside the rain is doing what Pacific Northwest rain does best: falling with the patient certainty of something that knows it will outlast every living thing it touches. My dog, a grizzled mutt named Finch, is snoring under the table with the serene confidence of a creature who has never once wondered what his purpose is. I envy him.

Between that whale and this rain-soaked evening, a lot of life happened in the usual chaotic, unrepeatable way. Marriages, a child, the death of parents, the slow realization that ambition is mostly a sophisticated form of fear. Books written and abandoned. Countries crossed. Mistakes made so spectacularly that they acquired a kind of grotesque beauty, like certain deep-sea creatures that glow only because they have nothing left to hide.

I have been thinking lately about scale. Not the kind you use in the bathroom, but the vertigo-inducing scalar difference between human time and planetary time, between a single human consciousness and the teeming, roaring, indifferent splendor of everything else. The whale taught me that first lesson. It surfaced, it breathed, and it returned to a world I could never visit. The rain is teaching me the same thing now, only more slowly and with better sound effects.

There is a peculiar loneliness that comes with loving the natural world too much. You begin to see every parking lot as a crime scene. Every plastic bag caught in a tree becomes a hanged man. You start measuring your own life against the lifespan of sequoias or the migration patterns of arctic terns and the comparison is rarely flattering. I have stood in old-growth forests in British Columbia where some of the trees were already ancient when Jesus was learning to walk. The feeling is not peace. It is closer to shame, mixed with an almost unbearable tenderness.

Yet I keep going back. Every summer I disappear into the mountains or the sea for weeks at a time. My wife long ago accepted that there are parts of me that will never fully come home. She says I smell different when I return—salt, woodsmoke, and something metallic like blood or ozone. I think what she’s actually smelling is relief. The version of me that pays taxes and answers emails is a costume. The one that sleeps on the ground and talks to ravens is closer to whatever I actually am.

Last August I spent ten days on a small island in the Broughton Archipelago with nothing but a kayak, a tent, and too many books. The orcas came through on the fourth day. A whole family—matriarch with her distinctive scarred dorsal, three adolescents, a nursing mother, and a calf so small it looked like a cartoon. They moved past my campsite at sunset with the unhurried dignity of royalty who have never needed to prove anything. I sat on a rock with my mouth open like an idiot. The calf spy-hopped less than thirty feet from where I was sitting and looked directly at me. I have never felt more completely seen. Not judged. Just seen. As if the young whale was saying, *Oh. One of those. How interesting.*

Then they were gone, and the water closed over them, and I sat there crying like a fool while the sky turned the color of ripe plums.

People ask me why I keep doing this—why I spend so much time in places where cell service dies and convenience is a memory. I never have a good answer. The truth is too large for language. It has something to do with being a temporary arrangement of atoms that, for a brief moment, gets to look at other temporary arrangements of atoms and feel the unbearable strangeness of existing at all. It has to do with the way a heron’s footprints look in the mud at low tide, like small dinosaurs had a dance party and left before anyone could ask questions. It has to do with the particular sound rain makes on a cedar roof at 3 a.m. when you’re half-dreaming and half-praying that the roof doesn’t leak.

I have become suspicious of people who are never astonished. They tend to be the same ones who are certain about everything. Certainty is a kind of spiritual arthritis. It feels strong but it locks you in place. Astonishment keeps you supple. It reminds you that the universe is under no obligation to make sense to you, and yet here you are anyway, with your ridiculous human brain trying to knit meaning out of stardust and childhood memories and the way certain songs make your chest feel too small.

My daughter is nineteen now. When she was small she used to ask me where dreams go during the day. I told her they hide in the pockets of old coats in the back of closets. She believed me for years. Now she studies marine biology and sends me photographs of things she finds in tide pools—creatures that look like they were designed by committee on hallucinogens. Last week she sent a picture of a decorator crab wearing a living hat made of sponges and bryozoans. The caption read: “nature’s drag queens.” I laughed so hard I scared the dog.

She has inherited my tendency to fall in love with the non-human world. I worry about her the way all parents worry about their children’s tenderness. The world punishes tenderness with a kind of grim enthusiasm. But then I remember the whale and the orcas and the way the forest sounds after rain, and I think maybe tenderness is the only practical response. Everything else is just noise.

I am fifty-four now, which feels both ancient and completely arbitrary. My knees sound like popcorn when I stand up. I need reading glasses. There are days when the sheer amount of past behind me feels suffocating, like trying to swim while wearing a backpack full of wet books. But there are other days—clear, salt-scoured days—when the future feels like an unopened letter with my name on it in handwriting I almost recognize.

The stove is making that comfortable ticking sound it does when the fire settles. Finch has shifted positions and is now using my left foot as a pillow. The rain has changed its rhythm; it’s softer now, almost conversational. I keep thinking about all the things I haven’t written yet. Not because I want to be remembered—God, what a tiring ambition—but because there are still so many questions that feel worth asking poorly.

What does it mean to be a good ancestor? Not just to our biological descendants but to the forests, the rivers, the strange and ancient intelligences that share this thin film of life on a wet rock hurtling through space? How do we learn to want less without feeling deprived? How do we grieve for species that haven’t gone extinct yet but almost certainly will within our lifetime? How do we stay astonished without becoming paralyzed by wonder?

I don’t have answers. Only the sense that the questions themselves are a form of prayer. That sitting here in the warm light of a cedar house while the rain falls on an island in the Salish Sea is itself a kind of answer.

Outside, a barred owl has begun calling. The sound is equal parts ghost and velvet. I know from years of listening that there are two of them—mates who perform this nightly duet with the solemnity of opera singers who have never seen an audience. Their calls braid together in the darkness, creating territories of sound that no human map will ever capture.

I think about the whale again. How it moved through a medium I can only visit wearing clumsy rubber skin and compressed air. How its life contained depths I will never understand. How its death, when it comes—as all deaths come—will return its vast body to the deep where it will become first a feast, then a garden, then eventually a mineral memory in the bones of the seafloor.

We are all doing the same thing, really. Just on different timescales and with varying degrees of grace.

The owl calls again, closer this time. Finch lifts his head, ears swiveling like satellite dishes. For a moment the three of us—mammal, bird, and whatever I am—are listening to the same darkness.

I think this might be enough.

I think this might be more than enough.

(Word count: 2,512)

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
